The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango

This whole series is built around MongoDB and Mongoose. I won't explain much else here, since this chapter is just a table of contents. If you want the full map of where the series goes — from installing MongoDB, through CRUD, Mongoose validators, middleware, Mocha testing, and finally MongoDB Atlas — this is the page to bookmark.
(29) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - Connecting to Atlas MongoDB
The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango

We already signed up for MongoDB Atlas and played with MongoDB Compass. So the obvious next step is actually wiring it into our own project. In this post I'll show you how little code it takes to point your app at the remote database.

Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework with a lot of upsides, which is why so many people are picking it up. But in VSCode you often get "Unknown at rule @tailwind" or "Unknown at rule @apply" warnings — here's how to silence them.
The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango (28): Browsing Your Atlas Database with MongoDB Compass

In the last chapter we set up a remote MongoDB database over on Atlas. But now what? How do we actually use the thing? This chapter walks through connecting to your remote database with MongoDB Compass, the official GUI tool — copying the connection string, swapping in your password, and poking around your collections.
Forced to Eat Mango (27): Setting Up MongoDB Atlas Step by Step
Forced to Eat Mango as a Frontend Engineer

Next up, let me walk you through MongoDB Atlas. This post also records how to sign up for MongoDB Atlas — from creating your account to deploying a free cloud database and setting up your user and IP access.
Testing MongoDB Reads with Mocha: find, findOne, and Pagination

Now we move on to writing tests for reading data. We'll cover find, findOne, and the pagination concept using limit, skip, and sort. Along the way I'll show you a sneaky gotcha with comparing MongoDB ObjectIds, and how a single toString() call saves the day.
Forced to Eat Mango (25): Testing MongoDB Updates with Mocha
Forced to Eat Mango Frontend Engineer

We've spent the last few posts testing how to create records, so now it's time to move on to editing. This post walks through writing Mocha tests for updating MongoDB data with Mongoose — using set(), update(), findByIdAndUpdate(), and findOneAndUpdate(). I'll also point out a subtle mistake in how you assert that an update actually worked.
Copy Text to the Clipboard with JavaScript (a Few Ways to Do It)

In real-world dev you often need a button that copies a chunk of text — think coupon codes or order numbers. This post walks through the different ways to copy text to the clipboard in JavaScript, from the old document.execCommand approach (with input fields and Range objects) to the newer, cleaner Clipboard API.
Mocha + MongoDB, Part 1: Writing a Test That Actually Saves Data

We've already covered the basics of wiring Mocha up to MongoDB. So what's next? More Mocha tests, of course. This time we write a test that creates a user, assert that it actually saved using the hidden `isNew` flag, and then fix the annoying problem where every test run keeps piling up duplicate records in your database.
Mango Frontend Dev (23): Mocha Meets MongoDB — Wiring Up the Database Connection

We've already gone through the basics of Mocha, so now it's time to start writing the MongoDB part. But before we touch any MongoDB code, there's a bit of setup to do first — the initialization. In this post we connect to the database from inside Mocha using the `before` hook, and deal with the async gotcha along the way.
